themes.name: Dust, Myths & Lost Worlds
themes.tagline: History gets weird, gorgeous, and surprisingly personal — from Greek gods to crumbling attics.

items.title: Ancient Historian Answers Your Greek Myth Questions
items.description:
Odyssey, the Iliad, Hercules, Troy and Medusa, what are they really about? Classicist historian Daisy Dunn answers your most asked questions about ancient Greek myths. 

0:00 Introduction
0:42 What are Greek myths?
1:32 Where do Greek myths come from?
3:02 Are Greek myths real?
4:26 How were Greek myths used?
5:34 Did ancient people believe Greek myths?
6:38 What religion was Greek mythology?
7:06 How many Greek gods were there?
8:00 Who were the Titans in Greek mythology?
8:42 How was Athena bo
items.why_watch: Classicist Daisy Dunn tackles the questions people actually Google about Greek myths — not just what they are, but whether ancient Greeks genuinely believed them and how they functioned socially and religiously. It's structured around real audience questions, so it moves efficiently through the material rather than padding a lecture. Good entry point if you want substance without a full university course.

items.title: 10 Amazing Buildings That Once Were!
items.description:
Some buildings survive for thousands of years. Others - just as magnificent, just as important - disappear completely. In this video, I use AI to reconstruct 10 of history's most legendary lost structures. From Shakespeare's Globe Theatre to the fortress that sparked the French Revolution, these buildings shaped the world... then vanished. Discover what they looked like, why they mattered, and how they were destroyed. History you can finally see again.

00:00 Intro
00:54 Hanging Gardens of Babyl
items.why_watch: This video uses AI reconstruction to show what ten significant lost buildings — including the original Globe Theatre and the Bastille — actually looked like before they disappeared, pairing visuals with the history of why each one vanished. The reconstructions make it easier to understand the architectural and cultural scale of what's been lost. A genuinely useful visual supplement to history you may already know in outline.

items.title: Clearing out the Chateau Attic, the start of MAJOR renovation.
items.description:
Check out Wild’s refillable deodorant, body wash, lip balm, and hand wash— packed with natural nourishment, Vegan and Leaping Bunny Certified, and super effective. Go to https://shopwildrefill.com/howtorenovateachateau-2, use my code RENOVATE20, and get 20% off all products for a limited time only! Available worldwide. 🏰🕯️ Discover our Chateau Candle and Wallpaper Collection here https://www.lefleur.fr/
And follow us on instagram: https://www.instagram.com/le_fleur_honfleur

Bonus videos and exc
items.why_watch: Clearing a chateau attic sounds mundane until you see what accumulates over centuries in a French country estate — this functions as both a renovation diary and an accidental archaeology of previous occupants' lives. It's the kind of slow, satisfying discovery video that works well if you like process over polish. A good entry point to the channel if you haven't followed their renovation journey.

items.title: I Tested Different Victorian Menstruation Products
items.why_watch: Karolina Żebrowska goes beyond describing Victorian menstrual products and actually tests reconstructions of them, which shifts this from historical curiosity into something genuinely illuminating about daily life constraints women navigated. Her dry, unsentimental humor keeps it engaging rather than grim. It's the kind of history that makes you reconsider what 'getting dressed' used to involve.

themes.name: Stranger Than Fiction
themes.tagline: Canned cheeseburgers, Greenland power plays, and Netflix rabbit holes — reality is doing its best.

items.title: The Weirdest Foods Ever Put in A Can
items.description:
As a people, we have made some pretty great technological strides like the wheel, the printing press, and the camera.  One that maybe doesn’t get spoken about enough though, is canned food. And much like the aforementioned advances, we’ve done some pretty strange stuff with it. 

What's the strangest thing you have ever seen canned? What is your favorite? Let us know in the comments!

(Much like real history, this video was produced entirely by human beings.)

#cannedgoods #canning #weirdhistory
items.why_watch: A light, quick tour through the genuinely strange history of what humanity has decided to preserve in tin — think whole chickens, cheeseburgers, and things you'd never expect to survive a shelf. It's the kind of video that works best as a casual lunch break watch. No deep insight, but reliably entertaining and mercifully short at 10 minutes.

items.title: Why everyone wants Greenland
items.why_watch: In just over two minutes, Johnny Harris distills the geographic, strategic, and resource-based reasons why Greenland keeps appearing in geopolitical conversations — no filler, no padding. It's a rare case where the short format actually serves the topic, giving you exactly enough context to follow the news without a 20-minute explainer. Efficient and worth the two minutes.

items.title: A BREAKDOWN of ALL Clues, Symbolism, & the Ending of HIS & HERS EXPLAINED
items.description:
In this Netflix HIS and HERS Explained / His & Hers Ending Explained, Plot Twist Analysis, I deep-dive analyze HIS & HERS 2026, starring Jon Bernthal and Tessa Thompson. This Netflix series is a perfect blend of mystery, crime, and whodunit, exploring themes of marriage, justice, and vengeance. I believe this film is excellent in capturing the major themes of prejudice and social hierarchy through great writing, acting, directing and filmmaking.

Furthermore, in this HIS & HERS explained video (
items.why_watch: If you finished the Netflix series and felt like you'd missed something, this breakdown maps the specific clues and symbolic choices the showrunners embedded around themes of marriage, prejudice, and class. Lucas Blue focuses on what the ending is actually arguing, not just recapping what happened. Worth watching alongside the show rather than instead of it.

themes.name: Slow & Intentional Living
themes.tagline: Seeds, puppies, and creative restarts — the gentle art of tending to things that take time.

items.title: Smart Gardeners Sow These Seeds in Late Winter
items.description:
Enjoy a no-obligation, completely free trial of the Garden Planner here: 
https://www.GrowVeg.com/grow-planner-free

And for your completely free abridged version of Ben's book, click here:
https://www.growveg.com/grow-book-download

Think it's too early to start sowing and growing? Think again! In fact, starting now gives smart gardeners a super-early head start on the growing season. In this week's episode, Ben suggests some of his favourite crops to start in winter and demonstrates some simpl
items.why_watch: GrowVeg makes a concrete case for which seeds actually benefit from a late-winter indoor start versus which ones are a waste of your windowsill space. The advice is tied to real growing timelines, not just enthusiasm, so it's practical for anyone planning a vegetable garden. If you've ever started seeds too early and watched leggy seedlings collapse, this is worth your time.

items.title: We Adopted a PUPPY! (& other updates)
items.description:
Haaaaallo, my dear friends!!! I am BACK from my month-long break with a little surprise, hahaha. May I introduce: Pippin! Our new little girl and member of the family.
Along with this huge life event, I wanted to have some cozy chats about this next year, tour a potential workshop space, relay how grateful I am for the 54607th time.
So grab a blanket and a snack, get cozy and de-stress.
I love you all so ardently, and I cannot wait to see where this next year takes us.
Let's GOOO!!

Patreon: htt
items.why_watch: Rachel Maksy returns from a break to introduce a new puppy, tour a potential new workspace, and have an unscripted, warm conversation about where her creative work is heading. It's a low-stakes life-update video that rewards viewers who already enjoy her presence, though it offers little for newcomers looking for a project or tutorial. Think of it as checking in with a friend rather than learning something specific.

morning_soundtrack.title: The Rolling Stones are for the young
morning_soundtrack.snippet: You could look at Foreign Tongues, the Rolling Stones' 25th album, as a tour of the group's musical variations. But it mixes reliable fun and remarkable energy with a generous attitude toward aging.

morning_soundtrack.title: Hana Stretton – “Seagull Theory II” & “As It Was Before This”
morning_soundtrack.snippet: The British-Australian musical artist Hana Stretton specializes in ambient folk, seemingly merging samples, field recordings, and original tracks into soft smears of emotion. Her songs inspire warm comparisons to geniuses like Grouper and Claire Rousay, and she's gained a fervent supporter in Phil E

morning_soundtrack.title: Petey USA – “Save For The Horses”
morning_soundtrack.snippet:
Back in May, Petey USA continued to explore his mix of emo, heartland rock, and electronics on the new single "Kiss The City." He's since given us the bummed-out synth-pop slow-build "Like A Mantra," and now there's a third new self-produced track.
The post Petey USA – “Save For The Hors

morning_soundtrack.title: Hovvdy – “You Will Go Far”
morning_soundtrack.snippet: Texas duo Will Taylor and Charlie Martin, aka Hovvdy, don't make nostalgic music, but there's a sentimental time-shiftiness that runs through their songwriting. Their latest singles, "Blast" and "Try Try Try," from the forthcoming album Big World, are especially adept at keeping the atmosphere light

morning_soundtrack.title: ALBUM REVIEW: Cinder Well’s Austere ‘A Blooming Body’ Finds Fascination in the Everyday
morning_soundtrack.snippet: Amelia Baker delivers her austere songs with the sober bearing of someone imparting fateful news. For a decade the California native, who records as Cinder Well, has excelled at an unadorned brand of folk that rewards full attention, performing spare tunes in a gently commanding voice. Teasing deepe

morning_soundtrack.title: Echo and the Bunnymen Reveal First Album in 12 Years
morning_soundtrack.snippet: Apples for Isaac features contributions from late drummer Clem Burke

morning_soundtrack.title: Jacob Ungerleider :: Congratulations
morning_soundtrack.snippet:
Jacob Ungerleider's solo debut, Congratulations, finds unexpected lift in melancholy. Richly arranged yet remarkably unforced, these finely crafted pop songs seem to end every phrase on an updraft.
The post Jacob Ungerleider :: Congratulations first appeared on Aquarium Drunkard.

morning_soundtrack.title: The Waterboys’ Mike Scott Hasn’t Yet Done His Last Jam
morning_soundtrack.snippet: With the release of a box set that expands upon the Waterboys' most iconic album, frontman Mike Scott reflects as much as he continues to propel forward.

morning_soundtrack.title: “Mother of the Blues” Ma Rainey Is Restored to Full Glory
morning_soundtrack.snippet: Ma Rainey's Mother of the Blues: The Complete Paramount Recordings 1923-1928 is a treasure trove that fully lives up to both of Rainey's famous sobriquets.

morning_soundtrack.title: Lily Lane Embraces Her Sense of Place in “So Long, California”
morning_soundtrack.snippet: Like a gem dropped into a flute of champagne, Lily Lane never truly dissolved into her effervescent surroundings in Los Angeles, moving to New York.

morning_soundtrack.snippet: At the 2026 Wintergrass festival in Bellevue, Washington, the Fretboard Journal teamed up with filmers I Know We Should to capture some of our favorite artists (and talk gear). For this session, FJ favorites Grant Gordy and Joe K. Walsh perform “Verona” and “Moon River” A com

morning_soundtrack.snippet: ﻿﻿﻿﻿﻿﻿ Today on the podcast, we are joined by guitarist Louis Campbell, who talks to us about growing up playing traditional music, studying with Martin Simpson, his affinity for using the electric guitar like a piano, and much more. You can check out his original music via his duo Spafford Campbell

morning_soundtrack.title: Luthier on Luthier: Marco Bortolozzo
morning_soundtrack.snippet: ﻿﻿﻿﻿ For Episode 115 of the podcast, we’re joined by classical guitarmaker Marco Bortolozzo. In our conversation, Marco tells us about the mentorship that launched his career, and the story behind his signature Olympic ring rosette, inspired by a 19th-century Spanish guitar. We also discuss his neck

global_silver_linings.title: 30 Beluga Whales are Finally Being Rescued After 2 Years Waiting in Shuttered Theme Park
global_silver_linings.snippet: The US and Canadian governments recently approved a plan for the transfer of captive beluga whales which have been stranded for years at a closed theme park. Shuttered years ago when the captivity for intelligent marine mammals became too controversial, Ontario’s Marineland still contained 30 beluga whales in need of new, more ethical homes. Located […] The post 30 Beluga Whales are Finally Being Rescued After 2 Years Waiting in Shuttered Theme Park appeared first on Good News Network .
global_silver_linings.reason: After two years stranded in a shuttered theme park, 30 beluga whales are finally getting the rescue they deserve — a reminder that sustained public pressure and dedicated conservationists can move mountains, even for the most overlooked animals. This is what it looks like when people simply refuse to give up.

global_silver_linings.title: What went right this week: the good news that matters
global_silver_linings.snippet: Heath wins for El Salvador and Cambodia, progress for the global ocean treaty, and a new species discovery, plus more The post What went right this week: the good news that matters appeared first on Positive News .
global_silver_linings.reason: Amid the noise of daily headlines, there are always quiet wins worth celebrating — and a weekly roundup dedicated to finding them is proof that good things keep happening whether or not we're paying attention.

global_silver_linings.title: 15 ‘Expert Flirts’ share their can’t-miss advice to help shy people flirt
global_silver_linings.snippet: "Do not think of anything except them. When you look at them, focus on how much you like them." The post 15 ‘Expert Flirts’ share their can’t-miss advice to help shy people flirt appeared first on Upworthy .
global_silver_linings.reason: Real human connection still matters, and the fact that people are sharing genuine, low-stakes ways to reach out to one another is a small but warm antidote to an increasingly isolated world.

global_silver_linings.title: ‘Food Really Is Medicine’
global_silver_linings.snippet: This article was originally published by Stateline. About a dozen states offer “medically tailored meals” to people with conditions such as diabetes and heart disease who get their insurance through Medicaid. Such programs significantly improve the health of the people in them, according to a new study.
global_silver_linings.reason: Growing evidence that food can function as medicine puts meaningful health tools back in everyday hands, shifting power toward prevention and away from purely reactive care.

global_silver_linings.title: A cheap, homegrown catnip lotion could replace DEET for millions of Ugandans
global_silver_linings.snippet: BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM A lotion made from catnip oil matched DEET in field trials in Uganda, according to research presented at the Society for Experimental Biology conference in Florence last week. Catnip (Nepeta cataria), a common herb in the mint family, contains nepetalactone, the compound that makes cats go sideways. It also […] The post A cheap, homegrown catnip lotion could replace DEET for millions of Ugandans first appeared on The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News .
global_silver_linings.reason: Researchers have found that catnip — an easy-to-grow backyard plant — produces a lotion that rivals DEET at repelling mosquitoes, offering a cheap, natural, and locally sourced protection against malaria for millions of Ugandans. It's a beautiful example of nature quietly holding solutions we're only beginning to notice.

discovery_articles.title: Porcelain Room in Aranjuez, Spain
discovery_articles.snippet: Visitors to Aranjuez often come for its royal gardens and the grandeur of the Royal Palace of Aranjuez. Yet one of the palace's most astonishing treasures is tucked away in a corner room that feels more like a theatrical illusion than a royal chamber. Completed in 1765, the Porcelain Room was the first major masterpiece created by the Royal Porcelain Factory established under Charles III of Spain.

discovery_articles.title: Celestia in Laporte Township, Pennsylvania
discovery_articles.snippet: Today, visitors to Celestia will find little more than a forest, a few traces of old foundations, and a historical marker along Route 42. In the mid-1800s, however, this mountaintop was intended to become something far more ambitious: a sacred city built in preparation for Christ's return. Founded by religious leader Peter E.

discovery_articles.title: National Gallery of Armenia in Yerevan, Armenia
discovery_articles.snippet: The National Gallery of Armenia is the largest fine arts museum in Armenia and home to the most comprehensive collection of Armenian art in the world. Founded in 1921, the Gallery preserves more than 42,000 works of Armenian and international fine art and decorative-applied arts. Its collections include masterpieces by world-renowned artists such as Hovhannes Aivazovsky, Vardges Sureniants, Arshile Gorky, Marc Chagall, Wassily Kandinsky, Donatello, Tintoretto, and many others.

discovery_articles.title: Buzz Aldrin Used This Humble Felt-Tip Pen to Save the Apollo II Astronauts. It Just Sold for More Than $850,000
discovery_articles.snippet: In July 1969, a quick-thinking Aldrin used the writing instrument to rearm the engine arm circuit of the lunar module "Eagle" after the switch broke off during the  moon landing

discovery_articles.title: This Viking Longship Crossed the Atlantic Before Starring in Christopher Nolan's 'Odyssey' as Odysseus' Greek Galley
discovery_articles.snippet: Hollywood stars including Matt Damon went to rowing camp to learn how to operate the vessel for the new film

discovery_articles.title: This Meteorite Crashed Into a New Jersey Home in 2024. Now, Scientists Say It Contains Some of the Building Blocks of Life
discovery_articles.snippet: The Hillsborough meteorite belongs to a rare class of rocks from space, according to a new study. It holds amino acids and other organic compounds, as well as evidence of salty water

discovery_articles.title: Buckingham Palace's Royal Picture Gallery Revives the Salon-Style Hang, Doubling the Number of Masterpieces on View
discovery_articles.snippet: Charles III reportedly had a say in the stylistic switch, which rearranged and added artworks by the likes of Rembrandt, Rubens, Vermeer and Van Dyck

discovery_articles.title: Ancient Egyptian princesses knew their way around weapons of war
discovery_articles.snippet: Analysis of six mummies from ancient Egypt challenges the idea that the bows and daggers buried with some royal women were merely ceremonial.

discovery_articles.title: This “exotic weirdo” exoplanet has a rocky surface and an atmosphere
discovery_articles.snippet: Astronomers found a helium atmosphere around a rocky exoplanet — a good sign for the search for life outside the solar system.

discovery_articles.title: Thermodynamic Computers Go With the (Energy) Flow
discovery_articles.snippet: Today’s computers need safeguards against random energy fluctuations. Thermodynamic computers would put those fluctuations to use. The post Thermodynamic Computers Go With the (Energy) Flow first appeared on Quanta Magazine

discovery_articles.title: Why Am I Left-Handed?
discovery_articles.snippet: An invisible difference in 10% of humans poses deep mysteries in several fields at once. The post Why Am I Left-Handed? first appeared on Quanta Magazine

discovery_articles.title: We Know Simple Fluids Can Flow. Turns Out, Some Can Fracture.
discovery_articles.snippet: Researchers thought that what enabled complex fluids to break apart was their elasticity. But a crack in a nonelastic simple fluid has them questioning that idea. The post We Know Simple Fluids Can Flow.

discovery_articles.title: Will We Ever Find Alien Civilizations?
discovery_articles.snippet: Astronomer David Kipping discusses why claims of extraterrestrial life keep dissolving under scrutiny, why we need a more statistically grounded approach to searching for life beyond Earth, and why it’s rational to believe that we may be alone. The post Will We Ever Find Alien Civilizations? first appeared on Quanta Magazine

featured_read.title: Diseases of the Will: Neuroscience Founding Father Santiago Ramón y Cajal on the Six Psychological Flaws That Keep the Gifted from Living Up to Their Gift
featured_read.snippet: "Our neurons must be used ... not only to know but also to transform knowledge; not only to experience but also to construct."
featured_read.blurb: Santiago Ramón y Cajal — the man who mapped the architecture of the human brain — turns that same precision on the quiet self-sabotages that hollow out a talented life from the inside. If you've ever suspected your greatest obstacle is not circumstance but something more interior and harder to name, this is the essay to read with a cup of something warm and no interruptions.

garden_note.note: First light crept over the Zürich rooftops at 05:08 this morning, catching the lavender and ripening tomatoes in that particular gold that belongs only to high summer — step outside early and the garden is already humming with bees before most of the city has boiled its first coffee.
in_season: Lavender (Lavandula angustifolia) — cut stems for drying before the heat peaks
Courgettes — check daily; they double in size overnight in July
Runner beans — harvest young pods to keep the plants producing
Dead-head dahlias and roses to encourage a late-summer flush
garden_note.sky_tonight: After dusk, a slim waxing crescent moon sets in the west — look too for the Summer Triangle (Vega, Deneb, Altair) climbing high overhead as darkness finally settles.
garden_note.sun_times: First light arrives at 05:08, with sunrise at 05:44 and a long summer evening stretching until sunset at 21:19.

previous_puzzle.label: Fern's Morning Riddle
previous_puzzle.prompt:
I rise without waking, fall without sleep,
I paint the window gold before the house stirs.
I am loudest in silence, gone by your first cup.
What am I?

fern_data.greeting: While the bread is still warm and the coffee is finding its way into your hands, settle in — today's canopy stretches from Greek myths and Victorian medicine to beluga whales finally going home and an exoplanet with something to say.
fern_data.top_pick_title: Lost Worlds & the Hum of Living Things
